title: Returning to Work After Maternity: A Rage Inventory
dek: The morning you leave your baby to return to the office, your body remembers the separation as trauma. This is not a failure of character; it is a biological protest, a fierce and necessary rage that lives in the fascia, the jaw, and the breath.
bodyMarkdown:
## The physical sensation of leaving the baby for the first time to return to work

You wake before dawn, the room a dim hush of shadows and the faint, warm scent of baby—milk-sweet and faintly sour, like honey left in the sun too long. It clings to your skin, the fine down of their hair, the softness of their breath, and your lungs remember it even before your mind fully does. You cup the tiny body close, the heat pressing into your chest, the fragile weight a tether you never realized could be so heavy. The clock’s faint tick in the background is a metronome counting down the minutes until you must leave.

Your diaphragm tightens with a sudden, sharp constriction—like a fist closing low in your chest, just below the sternum. It’s an old, primal knot—the same one that clenches when you are afraid, when you are about to lose something precious. Beneath that, the psoas muscles on either side of your lumbar spine coil and stiffen, as if bracing your body for a fall. They contract reflexively, a hidden scream in the body’s architecture, a contraction that pulls your pelvis forward and makes your lower back ache with an unnatural rigidity. The tension in your psoas is not just physical but elemental—a deep, visceral protest against the unnatural separation you are about to enact.

You breathe shallowly, the air tasting faintly metallic in your mouth, a residue of adrenaline. Each inhalation is a slow, deliberate attempt to soften the diaphragm’s grip, but it clenches again, relentless. Your shoulders round forward, the trapezius muscles knotting like taut ropes. The skin over your upper back prickles as if anticipating cold, though the room is warm, and the baby’s breath still floats against your neck in a fragile mist.

The minutes stretch out, thick and sticky. Your hands, still scented with the baby’s milk and the faint musk of their skin, tremble subtly as you fold clothes or pack a bag. The muscles in your forearms tighten, the flexors contracting in a silent grip, as if holding onto the moment itself. The smell of the baby lingers on your fingertips—warm, slightly sour, a scent that stabs at your gut like a secret you cannot share.

You feel the unnaturalness of this act deep in your belly—a hollow widening in the solar plexus, the web of nerves just beneath the diaphragm. It’s not hunger or pain but a gnawing absence, a space carved out where your baby’s presence should be. The fascia there is taut, a thin membrane stretched tight across the hollow, whispering of loss and separation. Your body does not recognize this as normal; it registers it as trauma.

The air in the room shifts when you lift the baby from the crib for the last time that morning. The slight resistance of their tiny limbs, the soft give of their ribs beneath your palm, the rapid flutter of their heartbeat against your sternum—each detail etches itself into your nervous system. You cradle them close once more, feeling the rise and fall of their chest, the gentle press of their cheek against your collarbone. Your breath hitching, the tight clasp of your pelvic floor muscles a silent scream in your pelvis, holding back the flood of tears you cannot name.

As you prepare to leave, the weight of the baby in your arms feels both grounding and unbearable. The muscles in your neck strain as you turn to leave, the sternocleidomastoid pulling hard on either side like a leash. The smell of your own skin, mixed with the faint trace of the baby, hangs in the air—a reminder of what you are leaving behind, what your body wants desperately to hold and never let go.

You step away. The world shifts. The tight coil in your psoas unwinds just enough for you to move, but the memory of that tension remains lodged deep in the muscles, a silent witness to the fracture between presence and absence. Your diaphragm still clenches intermittently, a dull ache beneath your ribs, reminding you that this separation is not just a logistical event but a rupture in your very flesh. The body knows. The jaw remembers. The ache between your shoulder blades whispers the story your mind cannot tell.

You are leaving, and your body is furious.

## The commute and arriving at the office

You step onto the cracked pavement just as the sky bleeds a pale gray, the kind of cold that slips past your wool coat and settles deep into the trapezius muscles, right where they cradle your neck and shoulders. The morning air is thin and brittle, biting at the back of your throat as you inhale. It’s the kind of cold that makes your levator scapulae tighten reflexively—those small muscles that hitch up from your cervical spine to your shoulder blades, as if bracing you for an unseen blow. You don’t notice the tension at first; it’s subtle, a dull stiffness that rides just beneath your skin like a low hum, but by the time you reach the bus stop, it’s a clenched coil.

The bus arrives with a hiss of brakes and the smell of exhaust, a chemical tang that slips into the lining of your nostrils alongside the faint trace of someone else’s perfume—too sweet, too sharp. You stand pressed against the glass window, the cold seeping in through the thin fabric of your gloves, and your jaw clenches. The masseter muscle tightens with the effort to hold back the surge of frustration that rises like bile, bitter and hot. You imagine the office waiting at the end of this journey—fluorescent lights humming a relentless white noise, the stale scent of recycled air, the endless hum of keyboards and half-hearted small talk.

When the bus pulls up to the stop nearest your building, you step off onto the cracked concrete, now slick with a thin film of ice. The cold air wraps itself around your shoulders like a shroud, and the muscles between your shoulder blades seize, those rhomboids contracting as if trying to hold your body upright in the face of this daily onslaught. Your feet echo hollowly on the marble lobby floor, the sound swallowed by the cavernous space. The air inside is artificially cold, conditioned to a clinical 68 degrees, each breath a reminder that life here is something engineered, not lived.

Your skin prickles under the fluorescent lights. They burn a sterile blue-white against your retinas, making the backs of your eyes ache behind your closed lids. The trapezius tightens further, a sharp, unyielding band stretching from your neck to your shoulders, as if your body is trying to hold itself together against the relentless flicker. You feel the subtle twitch of the levator scapulae again, a tiny rebellion beneath the skin, a muscle that remembers the nights spent soothing a crying infant, the soft weight of your child against your chest. Now it contracts in response to a different kind of demand—the demand to perform, to smile, to act as if the weight of your exhaustion and rage can be tucked neatly away beneath a professional facade.

You straighten your spine, pressing your shoulder blades back and down, but it doesn’t last. The muscles rebel swiftly, knotted and tight. You sense the tiny muscles along your cervical spine stiffening—the multifidi, the semispinalis cervicis—subtle players in this drama of containment. Your breath feels shallow, caught high in the chest, as you cross the threshold and enter the cubicle farm. The office hums with quiet urgency, the buzz of fluorescent lights blending with the drone of computers and whispered conversations. The air smells faintly of burnt coffee and sanitizer, a sterile cocktail that clings to the fabric of your blouse.

You sit, and the chair’s hard plastic presses into your sacrum, a cold reminder that comfort is a luxury you cannot afford here. Your hands shake slightly as you log in, the knuckles white where your fingers curl tightly around the edge of the desk. The rage coils in your belly, a slow, simmering heat that presses against your diaphragm and ribs, a visceral protest against this daily masquerade. You are expected to behave as if everything is normal—smile at the right moments, laugh softly at jokes you don’t find funny, nod when asked if you’re “doing okay.” But your body knows otherwise. The trapezius, the levator scapulae, the multifidi—they all remember. This is not just tiredness. This is rage, raw and unfiltered, stored in the tension that grips your muscles, in the cold bite of fluorescent light on your skin, and in the bitter taste of pretending.

## Pumping at Work

You know the sharp, almost metallic click of the breast pump as it starts—an unyielding mechanical hum that fills the cramped bathroom stall, or the stale closet with its flickering fluorescent bulb that buzzes too loudly for you to think clearly. The breast pump is not just a machine; it is the sound of your body’s fury being industrialized, its rhythm a metronome counting out the seconds you are forcibly detached from your child, from your own natural rhythm. The suction cups cling to your breasts like icy clamps, the rubber flanges pressing cold against your skin, the plastic tubing winding like a tether to this alien device. Each pulse pulls at the delicate tissue of your mammary glands, the alveoli contracting beneath your skin, but instead of the sweet release of nurturing, there is this mechanical extraction, a transaction that feels both intimate and violently impersonal.

The breast tissue itself remembers this violation. The lobules, swollen and tender from nursing, are now subjected to a forced, regulated schedule that is dictated not by your body's needs but by the clock of your workplace. The fascia around the pectoralis minor tightens with each session, the muscles beneath your collarbones twitching involuntarily, a silent protest beneath your shirt. Your ribs, expanded and softened from pregnancy, feel constricted, as if the very cage that once expanded to cradle new life now presses inwards, a physical reminder of how your body is being commandeered by external demands. The smell of your skin—sweat mixed with the faint, sour tang of breastmilk—clings stubbornly to your clothes, a sensory marker of the hours you spend tethered to this ritual, often in solitude.

Gabor Maté writes about how stress imprints itself on the body, how the body doesn’t just hold trauma but is trauma. Here, the stress is not abstract; it is the unspeakable rage that pools in your breast tissue like stagnant milk, thick and hot. The breast is a site of nurture and nourishment, yet under the fluorescent glare of the office bathroom, it becomes a battleground. The oxytocin that normally flows during breastfeeding is blunted, choked back by the cortisol flooding your system. Your body knows you are under siege. The sympathetic nervous system is activated, your heart rate quickens, your breath shallow and clipped as you try to squeeze milk from a body that knows it is being coerced rather than comforted.

The closet or bathroom becomes a liminal space—neither private nor fully public—a place where your body’s needs are legislated by policies and stigma. The walls close in, coated with the scent of mildew and cleaning chemicals, your shoulders hunch forward to shield your chest, to hide the vulnerability of your body’s soft, leaking edges. Your trapezius muscles knot with tension, the weight of indignity pressing down along your spine. There is rage here, not just at the injustice of the workplace but at the betrayal of your own body, which is forced to perform on demand, to produce sustenance on a schedule that ignores your exhaustion, your grief, your longing.

This rage is not a moral failing; it is biology screaming through the tightly wound bands of your pectoral fascia, through the pulsing veins beneath your skin, through the clenched jaw that aches by the end of the day. It is the body speaking in its own language, a language too often dismissed or misunderstood. Your rage is the echo of your body saying, “I am here. I am alive. I am not a machine.” The breast pump, with its cold plastic and relentless rhythm, cannot silence that message, no matter how many walls you close yourself behind.

## The meetings and the emails. The jaw tension (masseter), the suppression of the animal body.

You sit at your desk, the afternoon sun thinning through the blinds, the air sticky with the scent of your own sweat and last night’s unwashed sheets. The clock ticks louder in this quiet room where your child’s laughter is replaced by the ping of incoming emails, each one a small demand, a tiny call on your already stretched attention. Your jaw tightens—masseter muscles clenched like a fist around a secret. That tightness is not incidental; it is the body’s stubborn declaration that something is wrong, that the animal inside you is being stifled.

Those meetings—oh, the meetings. The sterile conference rooms where your body is expected to sit still, polite, contained. Your masseters contract, grinding silently as you suppress the urge to roar or snap. Karla McLaren calls anger a boundary-restoring emotion, and here, in this fluorescent-lit cage, your anger is screaming to be heard. Not as a problem to be fixed but as a vital signal that your space, your time, your energy are being encroached upon. When you bite back that rage, you are biting down on your own survival instincts, the same instincts that once urged you to protect your newborn with ferocity.

The emails pile up like unread threats, each one a tiny jab at your exhausted nervous system. Your body remembers the endless nights awake, the feedings, the crying, the ache in your sacrum from bending over a crib. Now, it is being asked to perform mental gymnastics while your masseters grind into a knot behind your ears. This is not a failure of character; it is biology. The masseter muscle, one of the strongest in the body, clenches to hold back the animal impulse—to growl, to snap, to say “no more.” It’s your body’s way of marking a boundary that words on a screen cannot respect.

The suppression of this animal body—the urge to pace, to yell, to punch a pillow—throws your nervous system into a quiet chaos. The parasympathetic nervous system, which should cradle you in calm, is hijacked by chronic stress. Your breath shortens; your neck stiffens; your shoulders round forward as if to disappear. This is a nervous system state that demands serious medicine, not quick fixes or platitudes. This is where herbs like [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20) come in, not as a gentle tea party but as a skilled intervention for the trembling edge of overwhelm. Motherwort acts on the heart and nervous system, easing the tight grip of adrenaline and coaxing your jaw to unclench, your breath to deepen. It is a medicine for the overfired, the boundary-crossed, the body that remembers too well the strain of holding rage inside.

Alongside Motherwort, [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers its own quiet power. Known in Traditional Chinese Medicine as a nervine, Skullcap calms the restless mind and soothes the jittery nerves without dulling your alertness. It is the difference between numbing out and reclaiming control. When your masseters clench in the middle of a meeting, when your fingers hover over the keyboard ready to send a sharp reply but instead you swallow it down, Skullcap helps you hold space for that anger—to feel it fully without being consumed by it.

This is not about suppressing anger; it’s about making room for it in the tight quarters of your workday body. The jaw remembers every time you bite it back, every time you swallow the furious “no” that rises like bile. But your body also knows how to release, if you give it the right medicine and permission. The meetings and the emails are not just professional obligations; they are arenas where your boundaries are tested daily. Your masseters and your nervous system bear the brunt of this test, speaking in tension and tremors what you cannot say aloud.

Let the tension in your masseter be a map, not a condemnation. These muscles are telling you where your edges are, where your boundaries have been crossed, where your animal self has been forced to silence. Listen to them. Respect them. And if you can, support them with the serious medicine of herbs like Motherwort and Skullcap—medicine that works in the body’s language, not against it.

## An embodied practice for the working mother’s rage

You close the door behind you. The lock clicks like a distant drum, a slow finality that marks the end of the day’s performance. Your shoulders are heavy, the trapezius muscles tight, knotted where the neck meets the base of the skull. The air in your home smells different—warmed wood, faint traces of baby powder, a lingering note of lavender from the diffuser. This is where the work’s edges can soften, where the rage you carried in your belly can find its way out of the fascia.

Stand barefoot on the floor. Feel the cold wood or tile pressing into the pads of your feet. The plantar fascia, that thick band of connective tissue along the soles, is your first point of contact with the earth. Your rage has been held in here, too—rooted in the tension that curls your toes under in exhaustion, the silent grip of your arches trying to support more than you signed up for.

Begin by letting the knees unlock, easing out the hyperextension that’s become familiar after hours of standing or pacing. Soften the quadriceps without collapsing; the vastus medialis and lateralis settle like quiet flames under the skin. Take a slow, intentional inhale, filling the diaphragm fully, feeling it drop like a parachute inside your ribcage. You might notice the serratus anterior—a tiger of a muscle beneath the shoulder blade—trying to escape the cage of your rib ribs, tight from holding your baby, your laptop, your phone, your breath.

As you exhale, bring your hands to your lower ribs, the floating ribs that feel like they don’t belong, that flare out when you’ve been holding your breath or swallowing down words all day. Press gently, your fingers tracing the curve, pressing into the intercostal muscles. Say nothing. Just notice the tension there. The ribs want to close, but they have been forced open, exposed. This is the body’s language: your rage, your exhaustion, your unspoken demands.

Now, with your hands still resting lightly on your ribs, allow your jaw to unclench. Feel the masseter and temporalis muscles release. The jaw remembers every time you bit back frustration, every clenched syllable that never made it out loud. Let your lower jaw drop slightly, enough to feel space between the molars, like a secret gap opening in the night. This opening sends a signal through the fascia of your face, down your neck’s sternocleidomastoid, which has been taut from holding your head upright through long meetings and longer nights.

Begin to rock gently from side to side at your hips. The iliopsoas, that deep muscle that connects your lumbar spine to your femur, pulses beneath your skin, remembering the hours spent rigid in chairs, the rush between work and child, the tight coil inside your belly. Sway slowly, letting the pelvis tilt forward and back, then side to side, as if you’re swaying with the weight of the day, the weight of your fury.

As you move, breathe deeply into the belly, allowing the transverse abdominis—the deep corset muscle that hugs your abdomen—to expand outwards rather than hold in. Imagine your anger as a thick fog lodged in the connective tissue around your organs, the visceral fascia. Each breath softens the grip, loosening the knots you didn’t know you were carrying.

When you feel ready, place your hands over your heart, right where the pectoralis major and minor meet the sternum and clavicle. Feel the steady beat beneath your palm. The heart muscle itself can’t hold rage; it pumps life through you regardless. But the fascia around it—the pericardium and the surrounding connective tissue—knows every time your chest tightened in anxiety or frustration. Press gently, acknowledging the weight you carry.

Finally, let your arms hang loose by your sides. Shake your hands and wrists gently, like you’re flicking off the residue of the day. Notice the extensor muscles of the forearm ripple under the skin, the thin sheets of fascia that connect knuckles to elbow. This shaking is a release, a small rebellion against the stillness that cages your fury in.

Stand still again. Feel the full body—your feet rooted, your ribs open, your jaw soft, your pelvis fluid. Your rage is not a failure; it is a language written in muscle and fascia. This practice does not chase it away. It moves it, sends it traveling through the body’s intricate web, from the base of your feet to the crown of your head. The workday is over, but your body’s story is ongoing, and here, in the quiet of your home, you can begin to read it again.

## FAQ: Maternal Rage and Returning to Work

**Q: Why does my rage feel so intense and unpredictable when I go back to work after having a baby?**  
**A:** Your rage isn’t just anger; it’s a body-wide signal rooted in the visceral, muscular, and hormonal upheavals you’re navigating. When you return to work, your trapezius muscles might clench without your permission, the fascia around your ribcage tightens like a corset, and your breath shortens into shallow, anxious gasps. This isn’t just stress—it’s your nervous system trying to hold onto safety and control in a landscape that feels profoundly unsafe. Oxytocin, which softened your body’s edges during pregnancy and early motherhood, is now mingling with cortisol spikes triggered by deadlines, childcare logistics, and the relentless mental load. This biochemical cocktail can make your rage erupt in sudden, scorching waves, sometimes over things that feel small on the surface but are really the body’s way of saying: “You are overwhelmed, unheard, and too depleted.” It’s not unpredictability; it’s your body mapping the edges of your limits in real time.

**Q: Is it normal to feel rage even when I love my child and want to be at work?**  
**A:** Absolutely. Rage and love are not mutually exclusive—they are cohabitants of your system, often tangled and inseparable. Your jaw remembers the nights spent nursing, the unrelenting pelvic floor remembers the physical trauma of birth, and your diaphragm remembers the constricted breaths taken in moments of exhaustion. Rage is the body’s protest against the relentless demands placed on your personhood, which include, yes, the deep love and responsibility for your child and the desire for professional identity. You can love your child fiercely and still feel furious about the way your workday stretches you thin or how your body never feels fully yours anymore. This rage is a messenger, not a moral failing or a sign that you are a bad mother or employee.

**Q: How can I tell the difference between rage that needs expression and rage that might harm my career or relationships?**  
**A:** Rage that needs expression is the kind that nags at your trapezius or sits like a knot in your forearms, demanding to be noticed before it becomes a wildfire. It rises early, often in the quiet moments—the 4 a.m. when your pelvic floor is tight and your breath is shallow, or the 3 p.m. slump when your spine aches and your mind spirals. This kind of rage, once named and moved through, can be translated into clear boundaries and honest conversations. Rage that risks harm is often rage stuffed down so long it bursts unpredictably—cutting off your breath, clenching your fists so hard your knuckles whiten, or turning your voice sharp and brittle. The body tells you: if you catch the first stirrings—the heat in your chest, the tension in your masseter muscle—before it becomes a volcanic explosion, you can use somatic tools or herbal allies to shift it. When rage feels uncontrollable, your nervous system is screaming for a recalibration, not for suppression or shame.

**Q: Can herbal medicine really help with this kind of rage, or is it just a placebo?**  
**A:** Herbal medicine is far from placebo when it’s respected as the body’s partner, not a quick fix. Take motherwort leaf, for example: it speaks directly to the heart’s fascia, loosening the grip of tension that holds rage in place, while also calming the vagus nerve. Or skullcap, which soothes the nervous system’s overdrive, especially when your shoulders are hunched and your neck muscles tighten like wire. These plants are not magic wands but they are serious medicines that meet your body in its specific terrain. The smell of [Gaia Herbs Calm A.S.A.P.](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07H2QFMNR?tag=spankyspinola-20) steeping in hot water at 9 p.m. is not just calming—it’s a ritual that lowers cortisol and invites your jaw to unclench. This is not about feeling “nice” or “relaxed” superficially; it’s about tending the biological roots where rage lives. The body knows these plants, and they know the language of your muscles and fascia.

**Q: How do I hold space for my rage at work without it consuming me or risking my job?**  
**A:** Holding rage at work is a somatic practice before it’s anything else. Notice where rage sits in your body—maybe it’s the flexors of your forearm twitching under the desk or a hot pulse behind your eyes during meetings. Give it permission to be there without trying to shove it away or act on it immediately. Small rituals—like tightening and releasing your hands slowly, or pressing your fingertips into the fleshy web between thumb and index finger—can anchor you in your body without verbalizing the storm. If you have a moment alone, a few deep breaths into the diaphragm can flood your parasympathetic system and soften the tightening in your sternocleidomastoid muscles. Over time, this internal dialogue with rage builds resilience. When you do speak up—maybe about workload or the lack of childcare support—your words come from a place of embodied clarity, not reactivity. Rage held without shame is a fierce, loyal guardian that can protect your boundaries and preserve your selfhood.
